intro.snapshot_description: A deeply quantitative chapter where microscopic molecular motion is linked to macroscopic gas properties. Ideal gas equation PV = nRT = NkT, the pressure formula P = ⅓ρv²_rms, and the three molecular speeds — v_rms, v_mean, v_mp in increasing order — are the most-tested formulas, appearing in almost every NEET paper. Degrees of freedom and the equipartition theorem (KE per degree = ½kT) directly determine Cv, Cp, and γ for monatomic, diatomic, and polyatomic gases — a trio of values that NEET tests both directly and inside thermodynamics questions. Mastering the speed-order inequality v_mp < v_mean < v_rms and the rigid-diatomic f = 5 rule eliminates the two most expensive errors in this chapter.

expected_questions.description: Consistently 3 questions per paper; occasionally 4 when a graph question (Maxwell distribution or P-T graph) is added. Standard split: 1 numerical on molecular speeds or KE, 1 on degrees of freedom/γ, 1 on ideal gas law calculations. Van der Waals may appear as a single conceptual MCQ in alternate years.

time_required.description: Theory and assumptions 1 hr; ideal gas law with Boltzmann constant 1.5 hrs; pressure derivation and molecular speed formulas 2 hrs; degrees of freedom, equipartition, Cp/Cv/γ tables 2 hrs; real gases and van der Waals 1 hr; MCQ bank drill 1.5 hrs.

difficulty_level.description: Formulas are well-defined and directly testable. The challenge is the density of similar-looking formulas (three speeds, multiple γ values) requiring precise recall. Students who build a one-page formula table and memorise the degree-of-freedom → γ mapping will find this chapter highly scoring.

intro.most_asked_style: Numerical MCQ on v_rms at a given temperature or comparing v_rms of two gases at the same T; conceptual MCQ on which gas has higher v_rms or KE; formula identification for γ of diatomic vs monatomic gas; matching degrees of freedom to molecule type.
intro.biggest_trap: Writing the order of molecular speeds as v_rms < v_mean < v_mp (completely reversed). The correct order is v_mp < v_mean < v_rms. NEET provides all four orderings as options. Numerically: v_rms : v_mean : v_mp = √3 : √(8/π) : √2 ≈ 1.732 : 1.596 : 1.414, confirming v_rms is the largest.
intro.fast_win: Memorise the γ values: monatomic γ = 5/3, diatomic (rigid) γ = 7/5, triatomic linear γ = 4/3. These three numbers answer approximately 30% of all kinetic theory MCQs without a single calculation. Also memorise: KE per molecule = (3/2)kT; KE per mole = (3/2)RT.
intro.revision_friendly: Yes. All essential formulas fit on two flashcards: (1) ideal gas + pressure + three speeds + KE; (2) degree-of-freedom table + Cv/Cp/γ for each molecule type. A 45-minute pre-exam review of these two cards plus the van der Waals equation covers over 85% of testable content.

subtopics.subtitle: Four major blocks: the ideal gas model and its molecular interpretation (PV = nRT, Boltzmann constant, Avogadro's number), the kinetic theory derivation of pressure and the complete set of molecular speed expressions (v_rms, v_mean, v_mp), degrees of freedom and the equipartition theorem leading to specific heats and γ for all molecule types, and the real gas correction via the van der Waals equation.
subtopics.revision_tip: Before any kinetic theory numerical, identify which speed formula is needed (rms uses √3, mean uses √8/π, most probable uses √2) and whether the answer is per molecule (use k) or per mole (use R). These two checks prevent the most common calculation errors. For degrees-of-freedom questions ask: is the molecule monatomic (f=3), rigid diatomic (f=5), or polyatomic (f=6)? — NEET almost always means rigid diatomic.

topics.topic_name: Ideal Gas Law and Molecular Speeds
topics.topic_description: Derives the macroscopic ideal gas law PV = nRT from microscopic assumptions and extends it to PV = NkT (k = R/NA = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K). Establishes the complete set of molecular speed distributions: root mean square speed v_rms = √(3RT/M) = √(3kT/m), mean speed v_mean = √(8RT/πM), most probable speed v_mp = √(2RT/M). The speed ordering is v_mp < v_mean < v_rms always. All three speeds scale as √T and as 1/√M — so lighter gases and higher temperatures both shift all speeds upward proportionally.

subtopics.name: Ideal Gas Equation and Boltzmann Constant
subtopics.description: Ideal gas law: PV = nRT (n = moles, R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹ K⁻¹) and equivalently PV = NkT (N = number of molecules, k = R/NA = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K). At STP (T = 273 K, P = 1 atm) one mole of any ideal gas occupies 22.4 L and contains NA = 6.023×10²³ molecules. Gas density ρ = PM/(RT) — density is proportional to molar mass and pressure, inversely proportional to temperature. Useful form: P = ρkT/m (m = mass of one molecule). Mixture: total pressure = sum of partial pressures (Dalton's law). Temperature in ALL gas law calculations must be in Kelvin.

subtopics.name: Three Molecular Speeds and Their Ratios
subtopics.description: Root mean square speed: v_rms = √(3RT/M) = √(3kT/m) = √(3P/ρ). Mean (average) speed: v_mean = √(8RT/πM) = √(8kT/πm). Most probable speed: v_mp = √(2RT/M) = √(2kT/m). Ratio: v_rms : v_mean : v_mp = √3 : √(8/π) : √2 ≈ 1.732 : 1.596 : 1.414. Order: v_mp is smallest, v_rms is largest — always. All three ∝ √T (doubling temperature multiplies speeds by √2, not 2). All three ∝ 1/√M (oxygen with M=32 has speeds 4 times smaller than hydrogen with M=2 at same T). For oxygen at NTP: v_rms = 461 m/s, v_mean = 424.7 m/s, v_mp = 376.4 m/s. v_rms is used in pressure and energy derivations; v_mp is the peak of the Maxwell distribution curve.

topics.topic_description: Derives gas pressure P from first principles by computing the momentum transferred to container walls by N molecules in a cubic box of side L. Key steps: each molecule with x-velocity v_x transfers momentum 2mv_x per collision; collision frequency = v_x/2L; single-molecule force = mv²_x/L; sum over all N molecules and divide by area gives P = (1/3)(mN/V)v²_rms = (1/3)ρv²_rms. From this derives KE per unit volume E = (3/2)P, and kinetic energy per molecule = (3/2)kT = (1/2)mv²_rms, and per mole = (3/2)RT. The factor ⅓ arises from averaging over all three coordinate directions.

subtopics.name: Pressure Derivation and Energy Density
subtopics.description: Pressure formula: P = (1/3)(mN/V)v²_rms = (1/3)ρv²_rms. Kinetic energy per unit volume: E = (1/2)ρv²_rms, therefore P = (2/3)E — pressure equals two-thirds of translational KE density. KE per molecule: K = (1/2)mv²_rms = (3/2)kT. KE per mole: K_molar = (3/2)RT. Since KE ∝ T (Kelvin), doubling temperature from 300 K to 600 K doubles KE but multiplies v_rms by only √2 ≈ 1.41, not 2. Effects on pressure: P ∝ T at constant V; P ∝ 1/V at constant T; P ∝ N/V (number density). Relation between pressure and speed of sound: v_sound = √(γP/ρ); ratio v_sound/v_rms = √(γ/3).

subtopics.name: Mean Free Path and Molecular Collision Frequency
subtopics.description: Mean free path: λ = 1/(√2 π d² n) where d = molecular diameter, n = number density (N/V). λ is proportional to 1/(n·d²): decreases as gas density or molecule size increases. Collision frequency: z = √2 π d² n v_mean. At NTP average intermolecular distance ≈ 10⁻⁹ m. Mean free path is much larger than molecular diameter. λ increases when pressure decreases (density decreases) or temperature increases at constant pressure. Applications: viscosity, thermal conductivity, and diffusion all depend on λ.

topics.topic_name: Degrees of Freedom and Law of Equipartition
topics.topic_description: Defines degrees of freedom f as the number of independent coordinates needed to specify a molecule's configuration. Monatomic (He, Ne, Ar): f = 3 (translational only). Rigid diatomic (H₂, O₂, N₂ at NEET temperatures): f = 5 (3 translational + 2 rotational). Non-rigid diatomic (high temperature): f = 7 (adds 2 vibrational). Triatomic nonlinear (H₂O): f = 6. Equipartition theorem: each degree of freedom contributes ½kT of KE per molecule. From this Cv = (f/2)R; Cp = (f/2 + 1)R; γ = Cp/Cv = 1 + 2/f. NEET always uses rigid diatomic f = 5 giving γ = 7/5 = 1.4. Monatomic γ = 5/3; diatomic γ = 7/5; triatomic linear γ = 4/3.

subtopics.name: Degrees of Freedom for All Molecule Types
subtopics.description: Monatomic gas (He, Ne, Ar): f = 3 (three translational). KE per molecule = (3/2)kT; KE per mole = (3/2)RT. Rigid diatomic gas (H₂, O₂, N₂, CO): f = 5 (3 translational + 2 rotational about axes perpendicular to bond axis; rotation about bond axis is neglected for rigid model). Non-rigid diatomic at high temperature: f = 7 (adds 2 vibrational degrees). Linear triatomic (CO₂): f = 5. Nonlinear triatomic (H₂O, SO₂): f = 6. Solid: each atom has 3 vibrational modes each with KE and PE degrees = 6f total; molar heat capacity = 3R (Dulong-Petit law). NEET context: rigid diatomic f = 5 is used in all standard problems unless explicitly stated otherwise.

subtopics.name: Equipartition Theorem Specific Heats and γ
subtopics.description: Equipartition theorem: each quadratic degree of freedom stores (1/2)kT average energy per molecule = (1/2)RT per mole. Molar specific heat at constant volume: Cv = (f/2)R. Molar specific heat at constant pressure: Cp = Cv + R = (f/2 + 1)R (Mayer's relation). Ratio γ = Cp/Cv = 1 + 2/f. Summary table — Monatomic: f=3, Cv=(3/2)R, Cp=(5/2)R, γ=5/3≈1.67. Rigid diatomic: f=5, Cv=(5/2)R, Cp=(7/2)R, γ=7/5=1.4. Triatomic nonlinear: f=6, Cv=3R, Cp=4R, γ=4/3≈1.33. As f increases both Cv and Cp increase but γ decreases. For water (treated as solid): C ≈ 9R per mole. Cv = R/(γ-1); Cp = γR/(γ-1). These follow directly from γ definition.

topics.topic_name: Real Gases and van der Waals Equation
topics.topic_description: Introduces corrections to the ideal gas model for real behaviour: intermolecular attraction reduces effective pressure by a/V² (adds a/V² to measured P); finite molecular volume reduces available volume by nb (uses V-nb). Combined van der Waals equation: (P + a/V²)(V - b) = RT per mole. Constant a accounts for intermolecular attraction (larger a = stronger attraction); constant b accounts for finite molecular size (b = 4NA × volume of one molecule). At low pressure and high temperature real gases approach ideal behaviour. Unsaturated vapours obey gas laws; saturated vapours do not. Free expansion of a real gas causes temperature to decrease (Joule-Thomson effect) because molecules must overcome attractive forces.
topic_pills: Free expansion: T decreases for real gas

subtopics.name: Van der Waals Equation and Its Constants
subtopics.description: Van der Waals equation per mole: (P + a/V²)(V - b) = RT. Per n moles: (P + n²a/V²)(V - nb) = nRT. Correction term a/V²: effective pressure is less than ideal because intermolecular attractions pull molecules away from the wall before impact; adding a/V² to measured P recovers ideal pressure. Correction term b: effective volume is reduced by the excluded volume of molecules; b = 4NA × (4/3)πr³ where r is molecular radius. At very high pressure or low volume the b-correction dominates (gas is hard to compress). At moderate conditions the a-correction dominates (gas easier to compress than expected due to attractions at intermediate densities). Critical temperature Tc: above Tc the gas cannot be liquefied by pressure alone. Real gas behaviour: when van der Waals gas expands freely into vacuum, temperature drops because KE converts to PE against intermolecular attractions; ideal gas temperature unchanged in free expansion.

subtopics.name: Comparison of Ideal and Real Gas Behaviour
subtopics.description: Ideal gas assumptions violated in real gases: (1) molecules have finite volume → b correction; (2) intermolecular forces are non-zero → a correction. PV/RT = Z (compressibility factor): Z=1 for ideal gas; Z<1 at moderate pressure (attraction dominates); Z>1 at very high pressure (repulsion and finite volume dominate). Boyle temperature TB = a/(Rb): at this temperature Z≈1 over a wide pressure range. At low pressure and high temperature all real gases behave ideally. Saturated vapour obeys neither Boyle's law nor Charles's law because condensation occurs. Unsaturated vapour (below saturation) approximately follows gas laws.

topics.topic_name: Ideal Gas Law and Molecular Speeds
topics.topic_description: The foundational gas equations and the complete set of molecular speed expressions — the most directly tested formulas in the chapter.
topics.notes_condensed_description: PV = nRT = NkT; k = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K = R/NA. Three speeds: v_rms = √(3RT/M); v_mean = √(8RT/πM); v_mp = √(2RT/M). Ratio √3 : √(8/π) : √2. Order: v_mp < v_mean < v_rms always. All ∝ √T and ∝ 1/√M. Hydrogen v_rms = 4× oxygen v_rms at same T (M ratio = 16:1 so speed ratio = 4:1). At NTP O₂: v_rms = 461 m/s.
topics.how_to_revise: Write all three speed formulas in a single column. Mark which coefficient appears under the square root: 3 for rms, 8/π for mean, 2 for mp. Since 3 > 8/π > 2, the order of speeds is directly readable from the coefficient. Do 5 numericals where T or M changes and verify the √2 factor between doubling temperature.

expected_questions.description: One question nearly every paper on v_rms comparison between two gases or effect of temperature on speed. Occasionally a density or pressure MCQ from PV = nRT.

time_required.description: 45 min ideal gas law with unit checks; 45 min three-speed formulas with ratio drill; 30 min MCQ practice on speed comparisons.

difficulty.description: Formulas are straightforward; the main errors are wrong speed ordering and forgetting that doubling T multiplies v by √2 not 2. One hour of targeted drill eliminates both.

topics.scoring_focus: Speed ratio v_rms : v_mean : v_mp = √3 : √(8/π) : √2 — this single relation answers all ordering MCQs instantly. v_rms ∝ 1/√M directly answers 'which gas is faster at same T' questions.
topics.high_risk_area: Reversing the speed order and putting v_rms as smallest. Also: using v = √(2RT/M) for v_rms (that is v_mp). The coefficient 3 belongs to rms, 2 belongs to most-probable.
topics.best_practice_style: Make a three-row table: speed name | coefficient | approximate decimal | order rank. Memorise this table before any numerical. Do 5 timed MCQs comparing v for different gases at same temperature.
topics.priority_rule: High. Speed formulas appear in 1-2 MCQs every paper. Master these before moving to degrees of freedom.

topics.topic_description: The microscopic foundation connecting molecular motion to the macroscopic gas pressure — tested both as derivation understanding and as numerical application.
topics.notes_condensed_description: P = (1/3)(mN/V)v²_rms = (1/3)ρv²_rms. KE per unit volume E = (1/2)ρv²_rms; P = (2/3)E. KE per molecule = (3/2)kT = (1/2)mv²_rms. KE per mole = (3/2)RT. KE ∝ T (Kelvin) — doubling T doubles KE; v_rms increases by √2. Momentum transfer per collision = 2mv_x; collision frequency = v_x/2L; force per molecule = mv²_x/L; pressure = (1/3)(mN/V)v²_rms after averaging over all directions. v_sound/v_rms = √(γ/3).
topics.how_to_revise: Work through the 5-step pressure derivation once without notes: (1) molecule bouncing in box; (2) momentum change per collision; (3) collision frequency; (4) force from one molecule; (5) sum over N molecules and divide by area. Then verify the result P = (1/3)ρv²_rms. This one derivation gives all pressure and KE formulas.

expected_questions.description: One question per paper on KE per molecule at given temperature, or ratio of pressures/KE for two gases. Less frequently, the P = (2/3)E relation in a direct numerical.

time_required.description: 45 min derivation walkthrough; 45 min KE numerical problems including temperature doubling scenarios.

difficulty.description: Derivation logic requires understanding, not just memorising. The KE formula KE = (3/2)kT is numerically straightforward once the formula is fixed.

topics.scoring_focus: KE per molecule = (3/2)kT is directly plugged into MCQs. P = (2/3)E relation is tested as a direct conceptual MCQ. Doubling T → KE doubles, v_rms increases by √2 only.
topics.high_risk_area: Claiming that doubling temperature doubles v_rms. This is wrong — v_rms ∝ √T so doubling T multiplies v_rms by √2. KE doubles (KE ∝ T), but speed only increases by factor √2.
topics.best_practice_style: Write the energy cascade on one line: T doubles → KE doubles → v²_rms doubles → v_rms multiplies by √2. Recall this cascade on every temperature-change question.
topics.priority_rule: Medium-high. The KE = (3/2)kT result is directly tested and also supports the equipartition section. Spend 1.5 hrs then move to degrees of freedom.

topics.topic_name: Degrees of Freedom and Law of Equipartition
topics.topic_description: The core of NEET kinetic theory: mapping molecule type to f, then to Cv, Cp, and γ — the most frequently tested sub-section in this chapter.
topic_pills: Rigid diatomic f=5 always in NEET
topics.notes_condensed_description: Monatomic: f=3, Cv=(3/2)R, Cp=(5/2)R, γ=5/3. Rigid diatomic: f=5, Cv=(5/2)R, Cp=(7/2)R, γ=7/5=1.4. Triatomic nonlinear: f=6, Cv=3R, Cp=4R, γ=4/3. γ=1+2/f. Cv=R/(γ-1). Cp-Cv=R (Mayer). Equipartition: each degree of freedom = (1/2)kT per molecule = (1/2)RT per mole. As f increases γ decreases. Non-rigid diatomic f=7 (NOT used in NEET unless specified).
topics.how_to_revise: Build a 3×5 table: molecule type | f | Cv | Cp | γ. Fill from formula γ=1+2/f. Memorise the three γ values (5/3, 7/5, 4/3) because they appear in adiabatic equation PV^γ = constant in thermodynamics. Then verify: Cp - Cv = R for every row. Tables self-check — if Cp - Cv ≠ R, there is an error.

expected_questions.description: One reliable question per paper on γ for diatomic gas, or Cv/Cp given γ, or fraction of energy in translational vs rotational KE. Becomes 2 questions in years when thermodynamics is light.

time_required.description: 1 hr building and memorising the f-to-γ table; 45 min Cv/Cp problem set; 45 min mixed questions on energy fraction per degree and internal energy calculations.

difficulty.description: The formulas are simple but the γ values for different gases look similar and are easy to mix. Students who rely on the formula γ=1+2/f rather than rote memorisation are more reliable under exam pressure.

topics.scoring_focus: The three γ values — 5/3 monatomic, 7/5 diatomic, 4/3 triatomic nonlinear — are the key recall facts. Given any one of Cv, Cp, γ, f the other three follow in one step each.
topics.high_risk_area: Using γ = 5/3 for diatomic gas when γ = 7/5 is correct. This error flows into wrong adiabatic relations in thermodynamics. Monatomic γ = 5/3 is for noble gases only.
topics.best_practice_style: For every gas-property question, write f first, then derive γ = 1 + 2/f. Do not try to recall γ directly. The formula γ = 1 + 2/f takes 2 seconds and never fails.
topics.priority_rule: Highest priority in this chapter. Affects thermodynamics questions too. Allocate 25% of chapter study time here.

topics.topic_name: Real Gases and van der Waals Equation
topics.topic_description: The modification of the ideal gas model to account for intermolecular forces and finite molecular size — conceptually tested rather than numerically in NEET.
topics.notes_condensed_description: (P + a/V²)(V - b) = RT (per mole). a corrects for intermolecular attraction (reduces effective pressure). b corrects for finite volume (b ≈ 4 × NA × molecular volume). At low P and high T: real gas → ideal gas. Saturated vapour: does NOT obey gas laws. Free expansion of real gas: temperature decreases (work against intermolecular forces). Ideal gas free expansion: temperature unchanged. Compressibility Z = PV/RT: Z=1 ideal; Z<1 dominant attraction; Z>1 dominant repulsion.
topics.how_to_revise: Write the van der Waals equation. Identify which correction term does which job. Draw the Z-P graph contours for three temperatures and one ideal gas horizontal line at Z=1. Five minutes reviewing this graph makes conceptual MCQs immediate.

expected_questions.description: Zero to one question per paper; usually conceptual (which correction corresponds to molecular size, or why temperature drops in free expansion). Rarely a numerical application.

time_required.description: 30 min reading equation and understanding each correction term; 30 min conceptual MCQs on real vs ideal gas behaviour. Not worth more time than this.

difficulty.description: Almost entirely conceptual in NEET context. Knowing which constant corrects for what and the qualitative temperature behaviour covers all likely questions.

topics.scoring_focus: Knowing (1) a corrects attraction, b corrects volume; (2) free expansion of real gas cools while ideal gas does not; (3) saturated vapour does not obey gas laws — these three facts answer every likely NEET question on this topic.
topics.high_risk_area: Confusing which constant is which: a is the pressure correction (attraction related), b is the volume correction (size related). NEET asks this directly as a statement-based MCQ.
topics.best_practice_style: Associate mnemonics: 'a for attraction' (both start with 'a'); 'b for bulk size' (b is about molecular bulk). This two-word check takes one second under exam conditions.
topics.priority_rule: Low priority. Cover in 1 hour at the end of the chapter. Do not sacrifice time from degrees of freedom or molecular speeds for this topic.

topics.subtopic_name: Molecular Speed Order — v_rms > v_mean > v_mp (Not the Reverse)

mistakes.label: Writing v_rms < v_mean < v_mp (completely reversed order):
mistakes.description: The correct order is v_mp < v_mean < v_rms. v_rms is the LARGEST of the three speeds. Students who confuse 'most probable' with 'largest' flip the order entirely. Numerically: v_rms ≈ 1.73√(RT/M), v_mean ≈ 1.60√(RT/M), v_mp ≈ 1.41√(RT/M) — the coefficients directly confirm which is largest.

mistakes.label: Confusing v_mp with v_rms in the ideal gas pressure formula:
mistakes.description: Pressure P = (1/3)ρv²_rms uses v_rms only. Substituting v_mp = √(2RT/M) instead of v_rms = √(3RT/M) gives a pressure reduced by a factor 2/3. The pressure derivation requires the mean-square speed, which is v²_rms by definition — not v²_mp.

topics.example: At 300 K, for O₂ (M = 32×10⁻³ kg/mol): v_rms = √(3×8.314×300/0.032) = 484 m/s; v_mean = √(8×8.314×300/π×0.032) = 446 m/s; v_mp = √(2×8.314×300/0.032) = 395 m/s. Order confirmed: 395 < 446 < 484. NEET asks which is largest and provides 'v_mp' as the most attractive wrong answer.
topics.trap_framing: NEET typically phrases this as 'Arrange the following speeds in increasing order' or 'Which of the following represents the correct relationship?' NEET provides all four orderings as options, making it a guaranteed mark for students who recall the coefficient rule.

trap_question.stem: For a gas at temperature T, which of the following correctly shows the relationship between root mean square speed (v_rms), mean speed (v_avg), and most probable speed (v_mp)?
trap_question.trick: The coefficients under √(RT/M) are: v_rms uses √3 ≈ 1.73, v_avg uses √(8/π) ≈ 1.60, v_mp uses √2 ≈ 1.41. Largest coefficient = largest speed. So v_mp is smallest, v_rms is largest. Answer: Option A.

topics.quick_rule: Coefficient rule: 3 for rms, 8/π for mean, 2 for most-probable. Since 3 > 8/π > 2, the order is v_rms > v_mean > v_mp always. Equivalently: v_mp is smallest, v_rms is largest. One number — 3 > 8/π > 2 — gives the complete ordering.

topics.subtopic_name: KE Doubles When T Doubles but v_rms Increases Only by √2

mistakes.label: Claiming that doubling temperature doubles v_rms:
mistakes.description: v_rms ∝ √T, not T. Doubling T from 300 K to 600 K multiplies v_rms by √(600/300) = √2 ≈ 1.41, not 2. Students who write v_rms ∝ T will get a factor of 2 instead of √2 — a very common NEET error. KE = (1/2)mv²_rms ∝ T DO double when T doubles because KE depends on v², not v.

mistakes.label: Using temperature in Celsius instead of Kelvin in speed formulas:
mistakes.description: All kinetic theory formulas require absolute temperature in Kelvin. A problem at 27°C means T = 300 K, not 27. v_rms = √(3RT/M) with T in Celsius gives a completely wrong result. NEET consistently provides temperature in °C in problem stems to force a Kelvin conversion.

topics.example: v_rms at 300 K = 400 m/s. Find v_rms at 1200 K. Using v ∝ √T: v_new = 400 × √(1200/300) = 400 × √4 = 400 × 2 = 800 m/s. WRONG approach — claiming T quadrupled so v doubled is correct by coincidence here only because √4 = 2. For non-perfect-square ratios like 300 K to 600 K: v_new = 400 × √2 ≈ 566 m/s, not 800 m/s.
topics.trap_framing: NEET gives initial v_rms at one temperature and asks for v_rms when temperature is doubled or quadrupled. Options include the ×2 answer (wrong for doubled T) and the ×√2 answer (correct for doubled T). The factor-of-2 option is placed first and looks more natural.

trap_question.stem: The rms speed of molecules of a gas at 27°C is 200 m/s. What will be the rms speed at 927°C?
trap_question.trick: T₁ = 27 + 273 = 300 K; T₂ = 927 + 273 = 1200 K. v_rms ∝ √T. v₂/v₁ = √(1200/300) = √4 = 2. v₂ = 2 × 200 = 400 m/s. Option A is correct. Option C (800 m/s) is the trap answer for students who think v ∝ T. Also note: temperatures must be in Kelvin.

topics.quick_rule: Two-step check: (1) convert °C to Kelvin; (2) use v_rms ∝ √T, KE ∝ T. Speed scales as square root of temperature; energy scales linearly. To get new speed: v_new = v_old × √(T_new / T_old).

topics.subtopic_name: Rigid Diatomic Has f = 5, Not f = 7 — NEET Always Uses Rigid

mistakes.label: Using f = 7 for diatomic gas in NEET problems giving γ = 9/7:
mistakes.description: f = 7 applies to a non-rigid diatomic at very high temperature (3 translational + 2 rotational + 2 vibrational). NEET always uses the rigid diatomic model (f = 5) giving γ = 7/5 = 1.4 and Cv = (5/2)R. Using f = 7 gives the wrong γ = 1 + 2/7 = 9/7 ≈ 1.29 and wrong specific heats. No NEET question uses f = 7 unless explicitly mentioning high-temperature vibrations.

mistakes.label: Using γ = 5/3 (monatomic value) for a diatomic gas:
mistakes.description: γ = 5/3 applies ONLY to monatomic gases (He, Ne, Ar, Hg vapour). For H₂, O₂, N₂, CO — all diatomic — γ = 7/5. This error propagates catastrophically into adiabatic process calculations where PV^γ = constant: using γ = 5/3 instead of 7/5 changes adiabatic work by approximately 25%.

topics.example: For N₂ (diatomic, rigid): f = 5; γ = 1 + 2/5 = 7/5 = 1.4; Cv = (5/2)R = 20.8 J mol⁻¹K⁻¹; Cp = (7/2)R = 29.1 J mol⁻¹K⁻¹. For He (monatomic): f = 3; γ = 5/3 ≈ 1.67; Cv = (3/2)R = 12.5 J mol⁻¹K⁻¹. NEET places both values as options in the same MCQ.
topics.trap_framing: NEET asks for γ or Cv for 'a diatomic gas like H₂' or 'a gas whose molecules have 5 degrees of freedom'. The options always include both 5/3 and 7/5, and sometimes 4/3 and 9/7. Recognise the molecule type first then apply γ = 1 + 2/f.

trap_question.stem: For a rigid diatomic gas, the ratio of specific heats Cp/Cv is:
trap_question.trick: Rigid diatomic: f = 5 (3 translational + 2 rotational). γ = 1 + 2/f = 1 + 2/5 = 7/5. Option B is correct. Option A (5/3) is monatomic. Option D (9/7) is non-rigid diatomic with f=7 — not used in standard NEET.

topics.quick_rule: NEET diatomic = rigid = f = 5 = γ = 7/5. Monatomic = f = 3 = γ = 5/3. If in doubt, use f first and compute γ = 1 + 2/f. The formula is the safety net.

topics.subtopic_name: γ for Diatomic Is 7/5 — Applying Monatomic 5/3 to Adiabatic Relations

mistakes.label: Using γ = 5/3 in PV^γ = constant for an adiabatic process involving air or O₂:
mistakes.description: Air is approximately diatomic (N₂ + O₂) with γ = 7/5 = 1.4. Using γ = 5/3 = 1.67 from monatomic in adiabatic relations gives incorrect temperature and pressure ratios. This error is extremely common when students remember that γ = 5/3 is the 'standard' value and apply it universally.

mistakes.label: Forgetting that γ = Cp/Cv determines adiabatic slope, not just specific heats:
mistakes.description: γ controls the slope of the adiabatic curve on a P-V diagram (steeper than isothermal by factor γ). A student who knows γ values for static problems but forgets to apply them in PV^γ = constant or TV^(γ-1) = constant loses marks on thermodynamics questions that require correct γ.

topics.example: Adiabatic compression of air (diatomic, γ=7/5) from V₁ to V₂=V₁/2: T₂/T₁ = (V₁/V₂)^(γ-1) = 2^(0.4) ≈ 1.32. With wrong γ=5/3: T₂/T₁ = 2^(0.67) ≈ 1.59. The error is 20%, which changes the answer option entirely.
topics.trap_framing: NEET gives an adiabatic compression/expansion problem for 'a diatomic gas' and expects γ = 7/5. Distractor options are computed with γ = 5/3. Students who recall the correct γ get the right option; those who use the monatomic value pick a plausible-looking wrong answer.

trap_question.stem: A diatomic ideal gas is compressed adiabatically to 1/32 of its initial volume. If the initial temperature is 300 K, what is the final temperature?
trap_question.trick: Diatomic: γ = 7/5 = 1.4, γ-1 = 0.4. T₂ = T₁ × (V₁/V₂)^(γ-1) = 300 × 32^0.4 = 300 × (2⁵)^0.4 = 300 × 2² = 300 × 4 = 1200 K. Closest option: none of the above would prompt checking — but if option B were 1200 K it would be correct. With γ=5/3: T₂ = 300 × 32^(2/3) = 300 × 2^(10/3) ≈ 300 × 10.08 ≈ 3024 K — clearly wrong. Always identify molecule type before applying γ.

topics.quick_rule: Diatomic gas (H₂, O₂, N₂, air) → always use γ = 7/5 = 1.4 in ALL adiabatic relations. Monatomic (He, Ne, Ar) → γ = 5/3. Write the molecule type and γ value at the top of every adiabatic problem before starting.

grounding_summary: Page 584: Assumptions of kinetic theory — molecules are elastic point masses, negligible volume (0.014% of gas volume), random motion, no intermolecular forces. Molecules collide elastically with walls transferring momentum and causing pressure. Gas properties: no fixed shape, indefinite expansion, compressible.

Page 588: Pressure derivation — momentum change 2mv_x per collision; collision frequency v_x/2L; force per molecule mv²_x/L; total pressure P=(1/3)(mN/V)v²_rms=(1/3)ρv²_rms. KE density E=(1/2)ρv²_rms; P=(2/3)E. Three speed formulas: v_rms=√(3RT/M)=√(3kT/m)=√(3P/ρ). v_rms ∝ √T, ∝ 1/√M. At NTP for H₂: v_rms = 1840 m/s.

Page 593: Speed ratios v_rms:v_mean:v_mp = √3:√(8/π):√2 ≈ √3:√2.5:√2. For O₂: v_rms=461 m/s, v_mean=424.7 m/s, v_mp=376.4 m/s. Degree of freedom discussion: atoms in solids have 6 degrees; diatomic behaviour changes with temperature (translation only at very low T, rotation added at higher T, vibration at very high T). Definition: NA = 6.023×10²³ per g-mol. Real gas correction: effective volume = V-μb.

Page 608: Assertion-reason MCQs on degree of freedom and specific heats. Diatomic has FEWER degrees of freedom than monatomic is FALSE (diatomic has more: f=5 vs f=3). Mean free path ∝ 1/density and 1/pressure — both confirmed. Cp > Cv because at constant pressure heat goes partially to expansion work (confirmed by equipartition). Internal energy of ideal gas depends only on temperature (confirmed). Container in motion suddenly stopped: KE of bulk motion converts to thermal KE of molecules — temperature rises.

Page 613: Solutions confirming f=5 for diatomic (3 translational + 2 rotational). Calculation: n₁(3/2 R)(10) = n₂(5/2 R)(6) gives n₁/n₂ = 1. γ = 1+2/f formula used directly. For non-rigid diatomic f=6: γ=1+2/6=4/3=1.33. Cp=(f/2+1)R=(5/2+1)R=(7/2)R for diatomic. Water treated as solid: C = 9R (3 atoms × 3kT internal energy each giving ΔU/ΔT = 9R). Cv = R/(γ-1) derived from γ definition.

Page 618: Equipartition theorem: (1/2)mv²_rms = 3×(1/2)k_B T giving T = mv²_rms/(3k). v_rms derivation from v ∝ √T used in numerical: v at 500 K to v at 400 K ratio = √(500/400) = √5/2. Solutions illustrating Cp-Cv=R and γ=Cp/Cv calculations for various gas types.
grounding_excerpt: Every gas consists of extremely small particles known as molecules. The molecules of a given gas are all identical but are different than those of another gas.
Total pressure inside the container P = (1/3)(mN/V)v²_rms where v_rms = √(v²_average)
the pressure exerted by an ideal gas is numerically equal to the two third of the mean kinetic energy of translation per unit volume of the gas
A diatomic molecule has three translational and two rotational degrees of freedom. Hence total degrees of freedom f = 3 + 2 = 5.

sources.title: NCERT Physics Part II Chapter 13: Kinetic Theory
sources.why_used: Primary authoritative syllabus reference for NEET kinetic theory. Verified all gas law derivations, molecular speed formulas, degrees of freedom assignments, and specific heat expressions against OCR source content.

sources.title: HyperPhysics — Kinetic Theory of Gases
sources.why_used: Cross-checked pressure derivation steps, v_rms formula, mean free path expression, and equipartition theorem statement. Verified numeric values for molecular speeds of O₂ at NTP.

sources.title: Khan Academy — Ideal Gas Laws and Kinetic Theory
sources.why_used: Verified Boltzmann constant value k = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K, ideal gas law forms PV = nRT and PV = NkT, and the energy per molecule expressions (3/2)kT for monatomic translational KE.

key_facts: Ideal gas: PV = nRT = NkT; k = R/NA = 1.38×10⁻²³ J/K; R = 8.314 J mol⁻¹K⁻¹
Pressure from KT: P = (1/3)ρv²_rms; KE per unit volume E = (3/2)P; P = (2/3)E
v_rms = √(3RT/M); v_mean = √(8RT/πM); v_mp = √(2RT/M); order: v_mp < v_mean < v_rms
KE per molecule = (3/2)kT = (1/2)mv²_rms; KE per mole = (3/2)RT; KE ∝ T (Kelvin)
v_rms ∝ √T — doubling T multiplies v_rms by √2, not 2; KE doubles when T doubles
Monatomic: f=3, γ=5/3; Rigid diatomic: f=5, γ=7/5=1.4; Nonlinear triatomic: f=6, γ=4/3
γ = 1 + 2/f; Cv = (f/2)R; Cp = (f/2 + 1)R; Cp − Cv = R (Mayer's relation)
Mean free path: λ = 1/(√2 πd²n); increases with lower pressure or higher temperature at constant P
Van der Waals: (P + a/V²)(V − b) = RT; a corrects intermolecular attraction; b corrects finite molecular volume
Free expansion of real gas: T decreases (work against intermolecular forces); ideal gas: T unchanged

review.notes: All formulas grounded in OCR pages 584-623 (sampled every 5th page). Speed formulas, pressure derivation, degree-of-freedom table, and specific heat expressions confirmed against pages 588, 593, 613. NCERT, HyperPhysics, and Khan Academy cross-checked. Content stays within NEET Class 11 kinetic theory boundary. Rigid diatomic f=5 used exclusively as per NEET convention. Non-rigid diatomic f=7 noted but not used as primary content.
